title: Understand: wave properties
description: Check your understanding of wave properties in this set of free, standards-aligned practice questions.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Identify each periodic wave property described in the table.**

Property | Description
:- | :-
[[☃ dropdown 1]] | maximum change from equilibrium as a wave passes
[[☃ dropdown 2]] | number of wave cycles that pass a point in a given amount of time
[[☃ dropdown 3]] | distance from the beginning of one wave cycle to the beginning of the next cycle
hints: As a wave travels through a medium, it causes the medium to be temporarily changed from its [[☃ definition 1]]. The maximum amount that the medium is changed from equilibrium is the wave's **amplitude**.
A certain number of full wave cycles will pass by a point in a given amount of time. The number of cycles that pass in a certain time, for example in one second, is the wave's **frequency**.

A periodic wave has a repeating pattern, consisting of multiple wave cycles in a row. The distance from the beginning of one wave cycle to the beginning of the next cycle is the wave's **wavelength**.

The answer is:

Property | Description
:- | :-
amplitude | maximum change from equilibrium as a wave passes
frequency | number of wave cycles that pass a point in a given amount of time
wavelength | distance from the beginning of one wave cycle to the beginning of the next cycle

assessment_items.question_content:
**Complete the statements about periodic wave properties.**

* The distance from the beginning of one wave cycle to the beginning of the next cycle is called [[☃ dropdown 4]].

* The maximum change from equilibrium as a wave passes is called [[☃ dropdown 5]].

* The number of wave cycles that pass a point in a given amount of time is called [[☃ dropdown 6]].
hints: A periodic wave has a repeating pattern, consisting of multiple wave cycles in a row. The distance from the beginning of one wave cycle to the beginning of the next cycle is the wave's **wavelength**.

As a wave travels through a medium, it causes the medium to be temporarily changed from its [[☃ definition 1]]. The maximum amount that the medium is changed from equilibrium is the wave's **amplitude**.
A certain number of full wave cycles will pass by a point in a given amount of time. The number of cycles that pass in a certain time, for example in one second, is the wave's **frequency**.

The answer is:

* The distance from the beginning of one wave cycle to the beginning of the next cycle is called wavelength.

* The maximum change from equilibrium as a wave passes is called amplitude.

* The number of wave cycles that pass a point in a given amount of time is called frequency.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Match each periodic wave property with its description by dragging the tiles.**

[[☃ matcher 1]]
hints: A certain number of full wave cycles will pass by a point in a given amount of time. The number of cycles that pass in a certain time, for example in one second, is the wave's **frequency**.

A periodic wave has a repeating pattern, consisting of multiple wave cycles in a row. The distance from the beginning of one wave cycle to the beginning of the next cycle is the wave's **wavelength**.

As a wave travels through a medium, it causes the medium to be temporarily changed from its [[☃ definition 1]]. The maximum amount that the medium is changed from equilibrium is the wave's **amplitude**.

The answer is:

Property | Description
:- | :-
frequency | number of wave cycles that pass a point in a given amount of time
wavelength | distance from the beginning of one wave cycle to the beginning of the next cycle
amplitude | maximum change from equilibrium as a wave passes

right: number of wave cycles that pass a point in a given amount of time
distance from the beginning of one wave cycle to the beginning of the next cycle
maximum change from equilibrium as a wave passes

assessment_items.question_content:
A periodic wave is traveling to the right. A snapshot of the wave is modeled below, along with some numbered measurements.

 [[☃ image 2]]

**Complete the statements.**

* The wave's amplitude is represented by measurement [[☃ dropdown 1]].

* The wave's wavelength is represented by measurement [[☃ dropdown 2]].
hints: As a wave travels through a medium, it causes the medium to be temporarily changed from its [[☃ definition 2]]. The maximum amount that the medium is changed from equilibrium is the wave's **amplitude**.

On the model, the dashed line represents the equilibrium level. The wave's amplitude can be measured from the equilibrium level to a crest, or from the equilibrium level to a trough.

The only numbered measurement that fits this description is $5.$ It measures the difference between the equilibrium level and the troughs.

(Measurement $3$ measures the difference between crests and troughs. This value is *two times* the amplitude.)

A periodic wave has a repeating pattern, consisting of multiple wave cycles in a row. The distance from the beginning of one wave cycle to the beginning of the next cycle is the wave's **wavelength**.

The wave's wavelength can be measured from a crest to the next crest, or from a trough to the next trough.

The only numbered measurement that fits this description is $2.$ It measures the distance from one crest to the next crest.

(Measurement $1$ measures the distance of two wave cycles. This value is *two times* the wavelength. Measurement $4$ measures the distance of three wave cycles. This value is *three times* the wavelength.)

The answer is:

* The wave's amplitude is represented by measurement $5.$

* The wave's wavelength is represented by measurement $2.$

options.alt: Diagram of a periodic wave. Measurement 1 points horizontally from the first crest to the third crest. Measurement 2 points horizontally from the first crest to the second crest. Measurement 3 points vertically from the level of the crests to the level of the toughs. Measurement 4 points horizontally from the first crest to the fourth crest. Measurement 5 points vertically from the equilibrium level running through the middle of the wave to the level of the troughs.

assessment_items.question_content:
A periodic wave is traveling to the right. A snapshot of the wave is modeled below, along with some numbered measurements.

 [[☃ image 2]]

**Complete the statements.**

* The wave's amplitude is represented by measurement [[☃ dropdown 1]].

* The wave's wavelength is represented by measurement [[☃ dropdown 2]].
hints: As a wave travels through a medium, it causes the medium to be temporarily changed from its [[☃ definition 1]]. The maximum amount that the medium is changed from equilibrium is the wave's **amplitude**.

On the model, the dashed line represents the equilibrium level. The wave's amplitude can be measured from the equilibrium level to a crest, or from the equilibrium level to a trough.

The only numbered measurement that fits this description is $2.$ It measures the difference between the equilibrium level and the crests.

(Measurement $3$ measures the difference between crests and troughs. This value is *two times* the amplitude.)

A periodic wave has a repeating pattern, consisting of multiple wave cycles in a row. The distance from the beginning of one wave cycle to the beginning of the next cycle is the wave's **wavelength**.

The wave's wavelength can be measured from a crest to the next crest, or from a trough to the next trough.

The only numbered measurement that fits this description is $4.$ It measures the distance from one trough to the next trough.

(Measurement $1$ measures the distance of two wave cycles. This value is *two times* the wavelength. Measurement $5$ measures the distance of three wave cycles. This value is *three times* the wavelength.)

The answer is:

* The wave's amplitude is represented by measurement $2.$

* The wave's wavelength is represented by measurement $4.$

options.alt: Diagram of a periodic wave. Measurement 1 points horizontally from the second crest to the fourth crest. Measurement 2 points vertically from the level of the crests to the equilibrium level. Measurement 3 points vertically from the level of the crests to the level of the toughs. Measurement 4 points horizontally from the first trough to the second trough. Measurement 5 points horizontally from the first crest to the fourth crest.

assessment_items.question_content:
A periodic wave is traveling to the right. A snapshot of the wave is modeled below, along with some numbered measurements.

 [[☃ image 2]]

**Complete the statements.**

* The wave's amplitude is represented by measurement [[☃ dropdown 1]].

* The wave's wavelength is represented by measurement [[☃ dropdown 2]].
hints: As a wave travels through a medium, it causes the medium to be temporarily changed from its [[☃ definition 1]]. The maximum amount that the medium is changed from equilibrium is the wave's **amplitude**.

On the model, the dashed line represents the equilibrium level. The wave's amplitude can be measured from the equilibrium level to a crest, or from the equilibrium level to a trough.

The only numbered measurement that fits this description is $4.$ It measures the difference between the equilibrium level and the troughs.

(Measurement $3$ measures the difference between crests and troughs. This value is *two times* the amplitude.)

A periodic wave has a repeating pattern, consisting of multiple wave cycles in a row. The distance from the beginning of one wave cycle to the beginning of the next cycle is the wave's **wavelength**.

The wave's wavelength can be measured from a crest to the next crest, or from a trough to the next trough.

The only numbered measurement that fits this description is $1.$ It measures the distance from one crest to the next crest.

(Measurement $2$ measures the distance of two wave cycles. This value is *two times* the wavelength. Measurement $5$ measures the distance of one-half of a wave cycle. This value is the wavelength *divided by two*.)

The answer is:

* The wave's amplitude is represented by measurement $4.$

* The wave's wavelength is represented by measurement $1.$

options.alt: Diagram of a periodic wave. Measurement 1 points horizontally from the second crest to the third crest. Measurement 2 points horizontally from the first crest to the third crest. Measurement 3 points vertically from the level of the crests to the level of the toughs. Measurement 4 points vertically from the equilibrium level to the level of the troughs. Measurement 5 points horizontally from where the wave crosses the equilibrium level to the next position where the wave crosses the equilibrium level.

assessment_items.question_content:
A periodic wave travels outward from a source. The wave's frequency can be changed. The speed that the wave moves forward does not change.

**Complete the statement.**

* If the frequency of the wave is increased, its wavelength will [[☃ dropdown 1]].
hints: The number of cycles that pass a point in a certain time, for example in one second, is the wave's **frequency**. If the frequency is increased, that means *more wave cycles* will pass a point every second.

We're told that the wave speed does not change. Each wave cycle moves forward at the same speed, no matter what the frequency is.

So if more wave cycles pass a point every second, the wave cycles must be *closer together*. In other words, the wavelength must have *decreased*.
If the frequency of the wave is increased, its wavelength will decrease.

assessment_items.question_content:
A periodic wave travels outward from a source. The wave's frequency can be changed. The speed that the wave moves forward does not change.

**If the wave's frequency is decreased, what will happen to its wavelength?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The number of cycles that pass a point in a certain time, for example in one second, is the wave's **frequency**. If the frequency is decreased, that means *fewer wave cycles* will pass a point every second.

We're told that the wave speed does not change. Each wave cycle moves forward at the same speed, no matter what the frequency is.

So if fewer wave cycles pass a point every second, the wave cycles must be *farther apart*. In other words, the wavelength must have *increased*.
If the frequency of the wave is decreased, its wavelength will increase.

assessment_items.question_content:
A periodic wave travels outward from a source. The wave's frequency can be changed. The speed that the wave moves forward does not change.

**Complete the statement.**

* If the frequency of the wave is decreased, its wavelength will [[☃ dropdown 1]].
hints: The number of cycles that pass a point in a certain time, for example in one second, is the wave's **frequency**. If the frequency is decreased, that means *fewer wave cycles* will pass a point every second.

We're told that the wave speed does not change. Each wave cycle moves forward at the same speed, no matter what the frequency is.

So if fewer wave cycles pass a point every second, the wave cycles must be *farther apart*. In other words, the wavelength must have *increased*.
If the frequency of the wave is decreased, its wavelength will increase.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Identify an appropriate unit for each periodic wave property.**

Property | Unit
:- | :-
frequency | [[☃ dropdown 1]]
wavelength | [[☃ dropdown 2]]
wave speed | [[☃ dropdown 3]]
hints: The number of wave cycles that pass a point in a certain time, for example in one second, is the wave's **frequency**. So, frequency is measured in units of cycles per second. This unit is called Hertz $(\text{Hz}).$

A periodic wave has a repeating pattern, consisting of multiple wave cycles in a row. The distance from the beginning of one wave cycle to the beginning of the next cycle is the wave's **wavelength**. So, wavelength is measured in units of meters $(\text{m})$ or other units of length.

A wave is a disturbance that moves through space. Any point on the wave, for example a crest, moves forward with some some **speed**. Speed is a measurement of distance travelled, divided by the time taken to travel the distance. So, wave speed is measured in units of meters per second $\left(\dfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}\right)$ or other units of speed.

The answer is:

Property | Unit
:- | :-
frequency | cycles per second $(\text{Hz})$
wavelength | meters $(\text{m})$
speed | meters per second $\left(\dfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}\right)$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Identify an appropriate unit for each periodic wave property.**

* Wave speed can be measured in units of [[☃ dropdown 4]].

* Frequency can be measured in units of [[☃ dropdown 5]].

* Wavelength can be measured in units of [[☃ dropdown 6]].
hints: A wave is a disturbance that moves through space. Any point on the wave, for example a crest, moves forward with some some **speed**. Speed is a measurement of distance travelled, divided by the time taken to travel the distance. So, wave speed is measured in units of meters per second $\left(\dfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}\right)$ or other units of speed.

The number of wave cycles that pass a point in a certain time, for example in one second, is the wave's **frequency**. So, frequency is measured in units of cycles per second. This unit is called Hertz $(\text{Hz}).$

A periodic wave has a repeating pattern, consisting of multiple wave cycles in a row. The distance from the beginning of one wave cycle to the beginning of the next cycle is the wave's **wavelength**. So, wavelength is measured in units of meters $(\text{m})$ or other units of length.

The answer is:

* Wave speed can be measured in units of meters per second $\left(\dfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}\right).$

* Frequency can be measured in units of cycles per second $(\text{Hz}).$

* Wavelength can be measured in units of meters $(\text{m}).$

The number of wave cycles that pass a point in a certain time, for example in one second, is the wave's **frequency**. So, frequency is measured in units of cycles per second. This unit is called Hertz $(\text{Hz}).$
